content:
# Luke Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 104-105 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_013_page_001.jpg, LUK_013_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And there were present some at that very season, reporting to him concerning the Galileans whose blood Pilate mixed with their sacrifices.

**2.** And answering, he said to them, "Do you think that these Galileans were sinners beyond all the Galileans, because they have suffered such [things]?

**3.** No, I say to you, but unless you repent, you shall all likewise perish.

**4.** Or those eighteen, upon whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them — do you think that they were debtors beyond all the men dwelling in Jerusalem?

**5.** No, I say to you, but unless you repent, you shall all similarly perish."

**6.** And he was speaking this parable: "A certain [man] had a fig tree having been planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking fruit on it, and did not find [any].

**7.** And he said to the vine-dresser, 'Behold, three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and I do not find [any]. Cut it down; why also does it render-useless the ground?'

**8.** And answering, he says to him, 'Lord, leave it also this year, until I shall dig around it and cast dung.

**9.** And if indeed it should bear fruit in the coming [year] — [well]; but if not, you shall cut it down.'"

**10.** And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbaths.

**11.** And behold, a woman having a spirit of infirmity [for] eighteen years, and she was bent-together and not able to straighten-up completely.

**12.** And seeing her, Jesus called-to [her] and said to her, "Woman, you have been released from your infirmity."

**13.** And he laid the hands upon her, and immediately she was straightened-up, and she was glorifying God.

**14.** And the ruler-of-the-synagogue, answering, being indignant that on the Sabbath Jesus healed, was saying to the crowd, "There are six days in which it is necessary to work; coming therefore on these, be healed, and not on the day of the Sabbath."

**15.** And the Lord answered him and said, "Hypocrites! Does not each of you on the Sabbath loose his ox or donkey from the manger, and having led [it] away, water [it]?

**16.** And this [woman], being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan bound — behold — [for] eighteen years, was it not necessary [that she] be loosed from this bond on the day of the Sabbath?"

**17.** And [as] he was saying these [things], all those opposing him were being put-to-shame, and all the crowd was rejoicing over all the glorious [things] being done by him.

**18.** Therefore he was saying, "To what is the kingdom of God like, and to what shall I liken it?

**19.** It is like a grain of mustard, which a man, having taken, cast into his own garden; and it grew and became into a great tree, and the birds of the heaven nested in its branches."

**20.** And again he said, "To what shall I liken the kingdom of God?

**21.** It is like leaven, which a woman, having taken, hid in three measures of flour, until the whole was leavened."

**22.** And he was going through cities and villages, teaching and making [his] journey toward Jerusalem.

**23.** And a certain one said to him, "Lord, are those being saved few?" And he said to them,

**24.** "Strive to enter through the narrow door, because many, I say to you, shall seek to enter and shall not be able.

**25.** From the [time] when the master-of-the-house should rise-up and shut the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock [on] the door, saying, 'Lord, open to us,' and answering, he shall say to you, 'I do not know you, whence you are.'

**26.** Then you shall begin to say, 'We ate before you and drank, and in our streets you taught.'

**27.** And he shall speak, saying to you, 'I do not know you, whence you are. Depart from me, all [you] workers of unrighteousness.'

**28.** There shall be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth, when you shall see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves being cast out outside.

**29.** And they shall come from east and west, and from north and south, and shall recline in the kingdom of God.

**30.** And behold, [there are] last [who] shall be first, and [there are] first [who] shall be last."

**31.** In that very hour, certain Pharisees approached, saying to him, "Go out and journey from here, because Herod wishes to kill you."

**32.** And he said to them, "Going, tell that fox, 'Behold, I cast out demons and perform healings today and tomorrow, and the third [day] I am perfected.

**33.** Nevertheless, it is necessary [for] me today and tomorrow and the following [day] to journey, because it is not possible [for] a prophet to perish outside of Jerusalem.'

**34.** Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the [city] killing the prophets and stoning those having been sent to her — how often I wished to gather together your children, in the manner a bird [gathers] her own brood under the wings, and you did not wish [it]!

**35.** Behold, your house is left to you. And I say to you, you shall not see me until it shall come [when] you say, 'Blessed [is] the [one] coming in [the] name of [the] Lord.'"

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **13:1 — "at that very season" (εν αυτω τω καιρω):** Sinaiticus reads εν αυτω τω καιρω, linking this episode directly to Jesus' discourse on reading "this season" (τον καιρον τουτον) in 12:56. The word καιρος here functions as a narrative hinge — the theological concept of discerning the decisive moment is immediately illustrated by a concrete event. The Galileans slaughtered by Pilate are otherwise unattested in historical sources, but Josephus records several incidents of Pilate's brutality against Jewish populations (Antiquities 18.55-62, 85-87). The mixing of blood with sacrifices implies the killings occurred in the Temple precincts during worship — a supreme act of sacrilege.

2. **13:2 — "sinners beyond all the Galileans" (αμαρτωλοι παρα παντας τους Γαλιλαιους):** Sinaiticus reads αμαρτωλοι παρα παντας. The preposition παρα with the accusative in comparative constructions means "beyond/more than." The assumption Jesus refutes is the doctrine of exact retribution — that extraordinary suffering indicates extraordinary sin. This is the logic of Job's comforters (Job 4:7-8; 8:20). Jesus does not deny that the Galileans were sinners, but rejects the calculus that their violent deaths prove them to be greater sinners than others.

3. **13:4 — "debtors" (οφειλεται):** Sinaiticus reads οφειλεται, "debtors," where verse 2 used αμαρτωλοι, "sinners." This variation is theologically significant: in Aramaic, the word חובא (hoba) means both "debt" and "sin" — an equivalence preserved in the Lord's Prayer (11:4, "forgive us our sins, for we ourselves also forgive everyone indebted to us"). The tower of Siloam is otherwise unattested; it was likely part of the fortification or aqueduct system near the Pool of Siloam in southeastern Jerusalem. The number eighteen appears in both illustrations, binding them together as a doublet.

4. **13:6-9 — The Parable of the Barren Fig Tree:** Sinaiticus preserves this parable unique to Luke. The fig tree planted in a vineyard follows normal Palestinian agricultural practice — fruit trees were commonly interspersed among vines. The three years (τρια ετη) of seeking fruit suggests a full probationary period. The vine-dresser's intercession introduces a theme of mediation absent from the owner's judgment. The proposed remedy — digging around (σκαψω) and casting dung (βαλω κοπρια) — represents intensive, deliberate cultivation. The sentence is left grammatically incomplete: "and if it should bear fruit in the coming [year] —" breaks off, leaving the positive outcome unstated but implied, while the negative alternative is explicit: "you shall cut it down" (εκκοψεις). This aposiopesis creates theological suspense — Israel's fate hangs on repentance.

5. **13:7 — "render-useless the ground" (καταργει την γην):** Sinaiticus reads καταργει, from καταργεω ("to render idle/useless/ineffective"). The barren fig tree is not merely unproductive — it actively depletes the soil, absorbing nutrients and water that could sustain other growth. The verb καταργεω carries strong Pauline associations (Romans 3:3; 1 Corinthians 13:11; Galatians 5:4) but here retains its agricultural concreteness: a fruitless tree is a parasitic presence in the vineyard.

6. **13:8 — "Lord" (κυριε):** Sinaiticus reads κυριε, the vine-dresser's address to the vineyard owner. In the parabolic framework, if the owner represents God and the fig tree represents Israel, the vine-dresser who intercedes functions as the prophetic/messianic mediator — Jesus himself, who pleads for more time and commits to greater effort. The phrase "leave it also this year" (αφες αυτην και τουτο το ετος) implies that prior years of patience have already elapsed.

7. **13:11 — "spirit of infirmity" (πνευμα...ασθενειας):** Sinaiticus reads πνευμα εχουσα ασθενειας, "having a spirit of infirmity." The genitive ασθενειας is qualitative — a spirit characterized by weakness/illness. Luke attributes the condition to a demonic spirit (confirmed in verse 16 where Satan is named as the one who "bound" her), yet the healing is described as a release (απολελυσαι, v. 12) rather than an exorcism. The woman was "bent-together" (συγκυπτουσα) — the participle describes a permanent condition of severe spinal curvature preventing her from standing upright (ανακυψαι εις το παντελες, "to straighten-up completely").

8. **13:14 — "ruler-of-the-synagogue" (αρχισυναγωγος):** Sinaiticus reads ο αρχισυναγωγος. This official was responsible for the orderly conduct of synagogue services (cf. Acts 13:15; 18:8, 17). His indignation (αγανακτων) is directed not at Jesus directly but at the crowd — he instructs them to come for healing on the six workdays, not the Sabbath. The irony is devastating: he treats divine healing as a form of prohibited labor (εργαζεσθαι), reducing God's liberating power to a scheduling problem.

9. **13:15 — "Hypocrites" (υποκριται):** Sinaiticus reads υποκριται in the plural, though Jesus is responding to one individual — the plural implicates all who share the ruler's reasoning. The argument from lesser to greater (qal va-homer) is characteristically rabbinic: if an animal may be loosed (λυει) from its manger on the Sabbath for mere watering, how much more should a human being be loosed (λυθηναι, v. 16) from Satan's bondage? The verbal echo between λυει and λυθηναι makes the argument explicit through wordplay.

10. **13:16 — "daughter of Abraham" (θυγατερα Αβρααμ):** Sinaiticus reads θυγατερα Αβρααμ, a phrase unique in the New Testament (cf. the masculine "son of Abraham" for Zacchaeus in 19:9). By naming her a "daughter of Abraham," Jesus affirms her full covenant membership — she is not defined by her infirmity or her gender but by her place in God's promises. The phrase "whom Satan bound" (ην εδησεν ο σατανας) interprets the illness as bondage, transforming the healing into an act of liberation. The eighteen years of bondage contrast with the immediate release — δει λυθηναι, "it is necessary [that she] be loosed" — expressing divine necessity that overrides sabbatical regulation.

11. **13:19 — "grain of mustard" (κοκκω σιναπεως):** Sinaiticus reads κοκκω σιναπεως, "a grain/seed of mustard." The mustard seed (sinapis nigra) was proverbially the smallest seed known in Palestinian agriculture. Sinaiticus reads εις δενδρον μεγα, "into a great tree" — an exaggeration, since mustard grows into a large bush, not technically a tree. The hyperbole is deliberate: the kingdom's growth exceeds natural expectation. The birds nesting in its branches echoes Daniel 4:12 (Nebuchadnezzar's tree) and Ezekiel 17:23, 31:6, where a great tree sheltering birds represents a kingdom providing refuge to the nations.

12. **13:21 — "three measures of flour" (αλευρου σατα τρια):** Sinaiticus reads αλευρου σατα τρια. The σατον (Hebrew seah) was approximately seven liters; three measures equals roughly twenty-two liters or about fifty pounds of flour — an enormous quantity, enough bread for over a hundred people. This is the same amount Sarah prepared for the three divine visitors at Mamre (Genesis 18:6). The woman "hid" (ενεκρυψεν) the leaven — the kingdom's working is initially invisible, pervasive, and transformative, penetrating the whole mass until all is leavened.

13. **13:22 — "making [his] journey toward Jerusalem" (πορειαν ποιουμενος εις Ιεροσολυμα):** Sinaiticus reads πορειαν ποιουμενος εις Ιερουσαλημ. This is one of Luke's periodic travel notices (9:51; 13:33; 17:11; 19:28) that structure the central section of the Gospel. The journey motif is theological rather than geographical — it represents Jesus' purposeful movement toward his passion. Everything taught between 9:51 and 19:28 falls under the shadow of the cross.

14. **13:24 — "the narrow door" (της στενης θυρας):** Sinaiticus reads της στενης θυρας. Luke uses θυρα ("door") rather than Matthew's πυλη ("gate," Matthew 7:13-14), and there is one door rather than Matthew's two roads. The verb αγωνιζεσθε ("strive/struggle") is from the athletic arena — it denotes the intense effort of an athlete competing for victory (cf. 1 Corinthians 9:25; 1 Timothy 6:12). The paradox is that many shall "seek" (ζητησουσιν) to enter but fail — seeking is insufficient without the agonizing effort of genuine repentance and discipleship.

15. **13:25 — "I do not know you, whence you are" (ουκ οιδα υμας ποθεν εστε):** Sinaiticus reads ουκ οιδα υμας ποθεν εστε, "I do not know you, from where you are." The phrase ποθεν εστε ("whence you are") concerns origin and belonging, not mere acquaintance. The master's ignorance is not literal but judicial — a formal disowning. Despite the excluded ones' protests that they ate and drank "before" (ενωπιον) Jesus and heard his teaching, proximity is not the same as relationship. Physical presence at Jesus' table and hearing his words in the streets does not constitute saving knowledge.

16. **13:27 — "workers of unrighteousness" (εργαται αδικιας):** Sinaiticus reads εργαται αδικιας, echoing Psalm 6:9 LXX (αποστητε απ εμου παντες οι εργαζομενοι την ανομιαν). Luke substitutes αδικια ("unrighteousness/injustice") for the Psalm's ανομια ("lawlessness"), a characteristically Lukan emphasis on justice. The command "depart from me" (αποστητε απ εμου) is a formal sentence of exclusion from the messianic banquet — the counterpart to the invitation to "recline" (ανακλιθησονται, v. 29).

17. **13:28 — "the weeping and the gnashing of teeth" (ο κλαυθμος και ο βρυγμος των οδοντων):** Sinaiticus reads ο κλαυθμος και ο βρυγμος. The definite articles (ο...ο) point to a well-known eschatological motif. The phrase appears six times in Matthew but only here in Luke. The grinding/gnashing of teeth (βρυγμος) expresses anguished rage rather than mere sadness — it is the response of those who witness their own exclusion while seeing the patriarchs and prophets inside the kingdom.

18. **13:29 — "from east and west, and from north and south" (απο ανατολων και δυσμων και απο βορρα και νοτου):** Sinaiticus reads this four-directional ingathering, echoing Isaiah 43:5-6; 49:12; Psalm 107:3. The universality of the gathering — from all compass points — implies the inclusion of Gentiles in the messianic banquet. Those who "recline" (ανακλιθησονται) take the posture of honored guests at a formal banquet. The reversal is complete: insiders (Israel) find themselves outside, while outsiders (the nations) recline with the patriarchs.

19. **13:30 — "last...first...first...last" (εσχατοι...πρωτοι...πρωτοι...εσχατοι):** Sinaiticus reads this chiastic reversal saying. The structure is A-B-B-A (last-first-first-last), creating a literary mirror that enacts the reversal it describes. This logion appears in multiple contexts (Matthew 19:30; 20:16; Mark 10:31), but Luke places it specifically after the banquet imagery, giving it an eschatological rather than merely ethical force.

20. **13:31 — "Herod wishes to kill you" (Ηρωδης θελει σε αποκτειναι):** Sinaiticus reads Ηρωδης θελει σε αποκτειναι. This is Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee and Perea (3:1), who had already beheaded John the Baptist (9:9). The Pharisees' warning may be genuine concern, a plot to drive Jesus away from their territory, or even a message from Herod himself designed to frighten Jesus into leaving. Jesus' response — addressing Herod as "that fox" (τη αλωπεκι ταυτη) — suggests he perceives manipulation behind the warning.

21. **13:32 — "that fox" (τη αλωπεκι ταυτη):** Sinaiticus reads τη αλωπεκι ταυτη. In Greco-Roman usage, the fox symbolized cunning and treachery; in rabbinic literature, the fox was contrasted with the lion as something small, insignificant, and crafty (cf. Song of Songs 2:15; Nehemiah 3:35 LXX / 4:3 English). By calling Herod a fox, Jesus simultaneously acknowledges his craftiness and diminishes his authority. The triad "today and tomorrow and the third [day]" (σημερον και αυριον και τη τριτη) is not a literal timetable but a Semitic idiom for a brief, determined period — Jesus' ministry has its own schedule, determined by divine necessity (δει), not Herod's threats.

22. **13:32 — "I am perfected" (τελειουμαι):** Sinaiticus reads τελειουμαι, present passive of τελειοω ("to bring to completion/perfection"). This verb carries sacrificial overtones — in Hebrews, τελειοω describes Christ's perfection through suffering (Hebrews 2:10; 5:9; 7:28). The passive voice indicates divine action: Jesus is being perfected by God through the course of his ministry, culminating in his death. The progression from "I cast out demons and perform healings" to "I am perfected" traces the arc from present ministry to future consummation.

23. **13:33 — "it is not possible for a prophet to perish outside of Jerusalem" (ουκ ενδεχεται προφητην απολεσθαι εξω Ιερουσαλημ):** Sinaiticus reads ουκ ενδεχεται, "it is not possible/acceptable." This is bitter irony: Jerusalem, the holy city, is the city that kills prophets. The verb ενδεχεται (a New Testament hapax) expresses a grim propriety — it would be unfitting for a prophet to die anywhere other than Jerusalem, because that is what Jerusalem does. Jesus claims the prophetic vocation and its inevitable consequence in a single sentence.

24. **13:34 — "Jerusalem, Jerusalem" (Ιερουσαλημ Ιερουσαλημ):** Sinaiticus reads the double vocative, a form of intense address expressing deep emotion (cf. "Martha, Martha" in 10:41; "Saul, Saul" in Acts 9:4). The lament combines accusation ("the [city] killing the prophets and stoning those sent to her") with anguished desire ("how often I wished to gather together your children"). The image of a bird (ορνις) gathering her brood (νοσσια) under her wings echoes the protective imagery of Deuteronomy 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Psalm 17:8; 36:7; 91:4 — God sheltering Israel under divine wings. Jesus claims this divine prerogative for himself.

25. **13:34 — "and you did not wish it" (και ουκ ηθελησατε):** Sinaiticus reads και ουκ ηθελησατε, the aorist of θελω — a decisive, completed refusal. The contrast between Jesus' repeated desire (ποσακις ηθελησα, "how often I wished") and Jerusalem's settled refusal (ουκ ηθελησατε, "you did not wish") is the theological crux of the lament. Divine desire and human will stand in tragic opposition. The verb θελω appears twice — once for Jesus' will, once for Jerusalem's — creating a collision of wills at the center of salvation history.

26. **13:35 — "your house is left to you" (αφιεται υμιν ο οικος υμων):** Sinaiticus reads αφιεται υμιν ο οικος υμων, "your house is being left to you." The present tense αφιεται ("is being left/abandoned") suggests a process already underway. The "house" (οικος) may refer to the Temple, the royal dynasty, or the nation itself — all three senses converge. Some manuscripts add ερημος ("desolate"), echoing Jeremiah 22:5 and 1 Kings 9:7-8, but Sinaiticus omits this word. The citation of Psalm 118:26 ("Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord") points forward to the triumphal entry (19:38), but also to a future eschatological recognition — Israel will not see Jesus again until they acknowledge him as the one sent by God.
